title: Bounded Arithmetic Meets Probability, and Applications in Cryptography
speaker: Jiatu Li
abstract:
The development of set theory in the 20th century was like the invention of a "mathematical telescope", through which we can observe all kinds of infinite sets and their interactions. In quite the opposite direction, bounded arithmetic serves as a "mathematical microscope", enabling us to understand and harness logical structures within elementary mathematics. 
The starting point of this talk is the bounded arithmetic theory PV (Cook '75), which intuitively captures the mind of a (weird?) mathematician who only works with Boolean strings and deterministic polynomial-time algorithms -- nothing else is allowed in theorems and proofs. This theory is "weak and strong" -- it does not seem to prove the Pigeonhole Principle, yet it can naturally formalize the celebrated PCP theorem. 
Interestingly, since this mathematician only accepts polynomial-time algorithms, probability over an exponential-sized support is not available. In this talk, I will cover classical and new approaches to extending PV with probabilistic toolkits. I will also discuss a somewhat surprising application: cryptography from the unprovability of mathematical theorems in these theories. 
(This talk will focus on high-level ideas; no prior background in logic is needed.)

proof_stages.label: PV as feasible bounded arithmetic
proof_stages.summary: The speaker locates bounded arithmetic below Peano arithmetic and presents Cook's PV as mathematics over finite strings with only polynomial-time functions and polynomial-time induction.

proof_stages.label: Approximate counting as the remedy for PV's probability gap
proof_stages.summary: PV cannot directly express ordinary circuit acceptance probabilities, so Jeřábek's APC_1 extends PV with approximate counting via surjective comparisons and a weak pigeonhole/range-avoidance principle to recover standard probabilistic reasoning.

proof_stages.label: Cryptographic target and unprovability-based theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines nonadaptive succinct noninteractive arguments in the CRS model and states that standard cryptographic assumptions plus an APC_1 unprovability assumption for an NP-versus-coNP/EF lower-bound proxy yield such arguments for NP.

proof_stages.label: Formalization-and-contradiction proof of the SNARK construction
proof_stages.summary: The proof formalizes a recent construction that assumes EF p-boundedness together with crypto inside APC_1, so that any nonuniform adversary breaking the proposed argument system, together with the true crypto axioms, would give an APC_1 proof of the forbidden lower-bound statement.

proof_stages.label: Weakening APC_1 while retaining probabilistic tools
proof_stages.summary: Because APC_1's weak pigeonhole principle appears computationally stronger than needed for approximate probabilistic reasoning, the speaker introduces a weaker subtheory that still counts definable sets and formalizes many standard tools while avoiding exact-counting consequences.

proof_stages.label: Proof-complexity reformulations and open program
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion and Q&A frame the remaining program: formalize more cryptography in weak theories, separate PV from the new theory and APC_1, and express the assumptions as propositional proof-complexity lower bounds supported by known unprovability results.
